coredata.dc:title: A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ by using amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Qi
Song, Jinping
Wang, Shangzhi
Yang, Jie
Guo, Yong
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized through acylation and amination reactions. The as-synthesized GQDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ was successfully developed. The detection limit can reach as low as 50nM. Moreover, the proposed sensing system was successfully employed to detect Fe3+ in real water sample, and satisfactory results were obtained. This work will open up new avenues to develop potential applications of GQDs materials in environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots
Sensing system
Fe3+ detection
Fluorescence probe

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the general strategy for amino acid-modified GQDs preparation and Fe3+ detection.

TEM images of AL-GQD (A), AS-GQD (B), GL-GQD (C) and the corresponding high-resolution TEM images (AL-GQD (D), AS-GQD (E), GL-GQD (F)). The diameter distribution of AL-GQD (G), AS-GQD (H), GL-GQD (I).

FTIR spectra of GO (black line), AL-GQD (blue line) and alanine (red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) XPS spectra of AL-GQD. (B) C1s high-resolution XPS spectra, (C) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra, (D) N1s high-resolution XPS spectra.

(A) UV–vis absorption (black line) and fluorescence emission (red line) spectra of the AL-GQD. (Inset) Photographic images of AL-GQD under visible light (left) and ultraviolet light (right). (B) PL spectra of the AL-GQD with different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Relative fluorescence intensity of AL-GQDs containing different metal ions. The concentration of metal ions is 2mM, λex =320nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of AL-GQDs in the absence and presence of different concentration of Fe3+. (B) Relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Fe3+. The linear plot of F0/F against the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 5.0×10−8–7.5×10−6 M (C) and 1.0×10−5–4.0×10−4 M (D).
Comparision of different GQDs fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.
Determination of Fe3+ in lake water by proposed method (n=3).

head.ce:title: A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ by using amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, and Institute of Applied Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi, 037009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at:College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, and Institute of Applied Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi, 037009, China. Tel: +86-352-7158185; Fax: +86-352-7158185.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Three kinds of amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized through acylation and amination reactions. The as-synthesized GQDs can emit strong blue fluorescence. The presence of Fe3+ will cause obvious fluorescence quenching of the three kinds of GQDs. Based on these, a general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ was successfully developed.

Amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized through acylation and amination reactions. The as-synthesized GQDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ was successfully developed. The detection limit can reach as low as 50nM. Moreover, the proposed sensing system was successfully employed to detect Fe3+ in real water sample, and satisfactory results were obtained. This work will open up new avenues to develop potential applications of GQDs materials in environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ is one of the most essential and abundant metal ions in the environment and in biological systems [1]. Fe3+ plays critical roles in numerous physiological and pathological processes, such as electron transport, enzymatic catalysis, nucleic acid synthesis, and cellular metabolism [2,3]. Deficiency or excess in Fe3+ may cause various diseases, such as anemia, Parkinson’s syndrome, Alzheimer’s disease, and cancer [4,5]. Therefore, a considerable amount of attention has been devoted in research to construct different analysis methods to quantitatively detect Fe3+ [6–11]. Among these analysis methods, fluorescence techniques, owing to their high sensitivity, simple operation, and excellent reproducibility, have been widely employed to detect Fe3+. Although accurate and sensitive, the above methods are usually complicated, time consuming, expensive, and generally not suited for on-site applications.

A great amount of effort has been recently devoted to explore the excellent selective and sensitive fluorescence probes for fast Fe3+ determination. Various functionalized organic chromospheres [12], conjugated polymers [13], and nanomaterials [14] have been employed to develop unique fluorescent sensors. However, most organic chromophores and semiconductor quantum dots are water insoluble and poisonous, which greatly limit their application. Thus, developing highly biocompatible and excellent water-soluble fluorescent probes is necessary to resolve this issue. Owing to their excellent performance and size-tunable optical properties, carbonaceous nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and fullerene, have extensively aroused the interest of researchers in recent years [15–18]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), being an exciting carbon nanomaterial, is becoming a potential new platform in designing and tuning fluorescent probes [19]. Compared with organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs exhibit superior properties, such as excellent photostability against photobleaching and blinking, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [20]. Additionally, unlike their cousins, i.e., carbon dots, GQDs clearly contain a graphene structure regardless of the dot size, which endows them with some of the unusual properties of graphene [21].

To date, several sensors for Fe3+ detection have been constructed based on photoluminescence (PL) of different GQDs. Wang et al. initially reported that GQDs can be used to detect Fe3+ based on the selective fluorescence quenching effect to Fe3+ (Table 1 ) [22]. GQDs obtained through one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of aspartic acid are also highly sensitive and selective for Fe3+ detection [23]. Furthermore, heteroatom doping can effectively modulate the intrinsic properties, including electronic properties, surface, and local chemical reactivity of GQDs [24]. For instance, Chen’s group developed a hydrothermal approach to synthesize N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs), which can effectively and selectively detect Fe3+ concentrations as low as 90nM [25]. N-GQDs obtained from hydrothermal carbonization of citric acid show higher quantum yield than GQDs, which has been used to detect Fe3+ in water [26]. Sun et al. [27] synthesized sulfur-doped GQDs (S-GQDs), which displays stable blue-green fluorescence, through one-step electrolysis of graphite in sodium p-toluenesulfonate aqueous solution. The S-GQDs showed drastically improved electronic properties and surface chemical reactivities compared with GQDs, leading to a sensitive response to Fe3+. In addition, chemical modifications using molecules with strong electron-donating or accepting ability can appreciably influence the electronic characteristics of GQDs [28]. Voelcker’s group [29] has successfully developed a turn-on orange-red fluorescent nanosensor based on rhodamine B derivative-functionalized GQDs for highly sensitive and selective Fe3+ detection. However, to date, most of the synthetic methods for GQDs require harsh conditions, such as strong acid/alkali/oxidant and high reaction temperature, which may limit the application of GQDs. Therefore, developing a general strategy to synthesize GQDs for Fe3+detection is necessary.

This work used a mild solution chemistry method to tailor graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets. Amino acid-modified GQDs were obtained through further amidation reaction. The as-synthesized GQDs displayed a uniform size and bright blue fluorescence. The presence of Fe3+ can cause obvious fluorescence quenching of the GQDs. Based on these, we successfully developed a general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ by using amino acid-modified GQDs (l-alanine modified GQDs (AL-GQDs), asparagine modified GQDs (AS-GQDs) and glycine modified GQDs (GL-GQDs)) as fluorescent probe.

Graphite powder was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Glycine, alanine, asparagine, dichlorosulfoxide, and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were acquired from Aladdin Reagents Company (China). Other reagents and metal salts used in the experiments were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagents Factory. All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments. Stock solutions of metal salts (1×10−3 M) were prepared by dissolving the metal salts in double distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino acid-modified GQDs fluorophores
ce:section.ce:para: The GO was first prepared using a modified Hummers method [30]. GO (50mg) was dispersed in 50mL of DMF and then sonicated for 2h. Dichlorosulfoxide (50mL) was added into the above dispersion and refluxed at 80°C for 36h. The mixture was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 10min to remove the supernatant. The product was washed twice with DMF and then redispersed in 70mL of DMF. l-alanine (66.6mg) (or l-glycine or asparagine) was added and reacted at 80°C for 48h. The reaction solution was naturally cooled down to room temperature, and the resulting yellow supernatant was filtered with a 0.22μm membrane. Furthermore, rotary evaporation was used to remove the bulk of DMF. The remaining yellow solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off of 1000) for 48h to remove the excess precursors and DMF. Finally, the light yellow aqueous solution containing GQDs was collected and stored at 4°C for further characterization and use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2100 HR transmission electron microscope operating at an accelerated voltage of 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a PHI-5400 electron spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Shuimadzu 8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by using Perkin Elmer Lambda35 UV/vis spectrometer. Fluorescences were measured on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Varian) by using a conventional 1cm×1cm quartz cell. The width both of excitation and emission slits were set to 10nm.

The quantum yield of the as-synthesized GQDs was measured based on a protocol described previously. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (quantum yield is 0.54 at 350nm) was used as reference standard. The quantum yields were calculated according to the following equation:
Φ x
=
Φ
s t d
I x
A x
A
s t d
I
s t d
η x 2
η
s t d
2
where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “R” refers to a reference standard with known quantum yield.

Fe3+ was detected in PBS (0.01M, pH=7.4) at room temperature. Different concentrations of Fe3+ solution were typically added into 1.5mL centrifuge tube containing 80μL of AL-GQDs solution, and then the mixture was diluted to 1mL with 0.01M PBS (pH=7.4). The corresponding fluorescence spectra were measured after reaction for 10min.

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra. The bands at 3420, 1717, 1261, and 1025cm−1 in the GO spectrum correspond to the stretching vibration of OH, CO, epoxy COC, and alkoxy CO bonds. The band at 1421cm−1 may have originated from the stretching vibration of carboxyl CO and bending vibration of OH bonds [37]. In the spectrum of alanine, the bands at 1650–1550cm−1 and 1450–1350cm−1 is caused by the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of COOˉ, respectively. The diffuse broadband at 3100–2000cm−1 is possibly caused by the stretching vibration of −NH3 +. As for the AL-GQDs, in addition to the vibrations of OH (∼3500cm−1) and CO bonds (∼1650cm−1), the stretching vibration of NH (∼3406cm−1) and CN bonds (∼1400cm−1), along with the bending vibration of CNH (∼1553 and 3060cm−1) and NH, were detected. Moreover, the CO stretching vibration shifted to low frequency. All these findings further demonstrated that alanine covalently attached to the GQDs. In addition, the band at ∼1630cm−1, owing to the stretching vibration of CC (aromatic rings), was observed both in the spectra of GO and AL-GQDs, suggesting that AL-GQDs will partially retain the sp2 characteristic structures of graphene [38].

XPS measurements were performed to further investigate the composition of GQDs. As shown in Figs. 3 (A) and S1–S2, the XPS of the three kinds of GQDs showed that the as-prepared GQDs contained mainly C1s peak (∼286eV), O1s peak (∼532eV), and N1s peak (∼401eV). The C:O:N atom ratios were 2.91:1.00:1.19 (AL-GQDs), 3.65:1.41:1.00 (AS-GQDs), and 6.11:3.12:1.00 (GL-GQDs). The high-resolution C1s spectrum of AL-GQDs (Fig. 3B) can be deconvoluted into four surface components, corresponding to CC/CC at binding energies of 285.1eV, CN at 285.5eV, COH at 286.6eV, and CO at 288.3eV. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3C), the two main components corresponded to the CO and COH/CO-C groups located at ∼531.5 and 532.5eV. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) further verified the presence of the CNC (399.7eV) and NH (401.1eV) groups. These results are consistent with the FTIR results.

Fig. 4 shows the absorption and PL spectra of the as-synthesized AL-GQDs. In the absorption spectra of AL-GQDs, an obvious absorption peak was observed at 305nm. However, for the AS-GQDs and GL-GQDs (Fig. S3, S4 in ESI), their absorption peaks were located at 285 and 300nm, respectively, which was attributed to n-π* transition of CO [39]. The PL spectra of AL-GQDs showed a maximum emission peak at 413nm under the excitation wavelength of 330nm. The AS-GQDs displayed a maximum PL peak at 443nm when the excitation wavelength was set at 320nm, whereas the maximum PL peak of GL-GQDs is located at 454nm (excitation wavelength is set at 360nm). In addition, with progressively increasing excitation wavelength (Figs. 4 B and S3–S4 in ESI), the fluorescence emission peaks of the three GQDs samples shifted to longer wavelengths, accompanied by a decrease in PL intensity. This excitation-dependent feature may be attributed to the distribution of emissive trap sites on each GQDs and to different particle sizes [40]. The fluorescence quantum yields were 12.67%, 11.27%, and 10.92% for AL-GQDs, AS-GQDs, and GL-GQDs, respectively (Table S1 in ESI). These values were comparable to those in previous reports [41]. The photographic images showed that three kinds of GQDs presented a light yellow color under visible light, whereas they produced strong blue luminescence when irradiated under 365nm UV light. This property is favorable for fluorescence-based analytical application.

The effects of pH and storage time on the fluorescence stability of GQDs were studied. Fig. S5(A–C) show the effects of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the three kinds of GQDs. For AL-GQDs and AS-GQDs, the fluorescence intensity was nearly constant at pH 2.0–11.0. However, the variation in pH would influence the fluorescence intensity of GL-GQDs. When pH is 2.0–7.0, the fluorescence intensity was nearly constant; however, the fluorescence intensity dropped significantly when pH is higher than 8.0. Figs. S5(D–F) show the effects of storage time on the stability of GQDs fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity and spectral feature of GQDs remained unchanged even after 10days, indicating that the as-prepared GQDs exhibit excellent stability, which is beneficial for preservation and transportation.

To investigate the selectivity of GQDs toward different metal ions, 200μM of metal ions, including Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, and Fe3+, was added into the GQDs solution, and the PL responses were recorded. Fig. 5 shows that addition of Fe3+ demonstrated the strongest fluorescence quenching effect, implying its potential as an effective fluorescence probe for Fe3+ detection. The obvious fluorescence quenching ability may be attributed to the special coordination interaction between Fe3+ and the carboxylic and amide groups of GQDs [42,43]. Fig. S6 shows the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of the system. The introduced Fe3+ rapidly reduced the fluorescence intensity within 1min, and then the fluorescence intensity plateaued. Thus, to obtain a stable fluorescence signal, 5min was chosen as the reaction time in the subsequent experiments.

Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence quenching spectra of AL-GQDs at various Fe3+ concentrations. The relative fluorescence peak intensity gradually decreased with increasing Fe3+ concentration. The dynamic response window was 5.0×10−8–4.0×10−4 M (Fig. 6B). Good linear responses between the relative fluorescence ratio (F0/F) and Fe3+ concentration were obtained within the range of 5.0×10−8–7.5×10−6 M and 1.0×10−5–4.0×10−4 M (Fig. 6C and D), respectively. The corresponding calibration curve can be depicted as F0/F=0.01124°C (μM)+1.0230 (r=0.9907) and F0/F=0.00227°C (μM)+1.1439 (r=0.9939). The detection limit was ∼50nM, which is comparable to that reported (Table 1).

Further investigation showed that both AS-GQDs and GL-GQDs exhibit excellent selectivity toward Fe3+. As depicted in Fig. S7, except for Fe3+, other metal ions cannot cause obvious fluorescence changes in GL-GQDs, suggesting their excellent selectivity toward Fe3+. However, with respect to AS-GQDs (Fig. S9), Cu2+ and Ni2+ ions can cause moderate fluorescence changes (defined as a relative change in fluorescence intensity by 40%–60% compared with the blank). Similar to AL-GQDs, the relative fluorescence ratio of the AS-GQDs or GL-GQDs showed good linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration. Regarding AS-GQDs, the linear equation is expressed as F0/F=0.00422°C (μM)+0.9940 (r=0.9956) within the range of 5.0×10−7–2.0×10−4 M, and the detection limit is 0.1μM. With regard to GL-GQDs, the linear equation is F0/F=0.00142°C (μM)+1.02691 (r=0.9878) within the range of 5.0×10−7–2.0×10−4 M, and the detection limit is 0.1μM. Table 1 shows the comparison between the proposed method and the earlier reported methods. Our proposed method apparently exhibited a very wide linear range and low detection limit compared with the earlier reported methods, except those presented in the work of Wang [22] and Li [27]. All these findings demonstrated that the as-synthesized GQDs possess excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ detection, implying that the developed general strategy for Fe3+ detection offers practical applications.

To investigate the applicability of the as-synthesized GQDs for Fe3+ detection in real water sample, we collected lake water samples from Wenying Lake of Datong in Shanxi Province (China). The lake water samples were filtered with a 0.22μm membrane. The filtered water samples were added into the AL-GQDs system, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded. Table 2 shows that Fe3+ concentration in the lake water was 180nM, which is higher than the detection limit. Recovery experiments were further performed by injecting standard solution containing different Fe3+ concentrations into the filtered lake water. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries range from 97.8% to 103.4%, whereas the relative standard deviations range from 1.6% to 3.1%. This excellent accuracy and reliability suggest that our method can be used for the practical Fe3+ detection.

The as-prepared products can emit strong and stable blue fluorescence at a quantum yield of 10.92%–12.67%, and the presence of Fe3+ could selectively and efficiently quench the fluorescence of GQDs. The typical hexagonal shape and zigzag edge revealed that the GQDs consist of graphene unit with a size distribution within the range of 3.0–6.0nm. In addition, GQDs showed good sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+. For AL-GQDs, AS-GQDs, and GL-GQDs, the detection limits were 50, 100, and 100nM, respectively. These results show that we have successfully demonstrated a general strategy for Fe3+ determination, which will provide broader prospects in their application in detection of Fe ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21301111), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province of China (2015011017, 2015021049), and Scientific and Technologial Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2015181), the Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi (OIT).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.08.039.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
